title: Two Pointers
description: Using two index variables moving through an array to solve problems in O(n) instead of the O(n^2) brute force from Big-O Notation.
objectives: Recognize when a two-pointer approach applies
Use opposite-direction pointers to solve a pair-sum problem
Compare the two-pointer O(n) solution against a brute-force O(n^2) one

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Brute Force is O(n^2)
Suppose we have a SORTED array and want to find two numbers that add up to a target. The obvious approach: check every pair.
This is O(n^2), exactly like the hasDuplicate example from Big-O Notation -- nested loops checking every pair. For a sorted array, we can do dramatically better.

A technique using two index variables moving through a data structure -- often from opposite ends inward, or both forward at different speeds -- to solve a problem in a single pass instead of nested loops.

sections.title: Tracing hasPairTwoPointers({1,3,5,7,9,11}, 12)
steps: left=0 (value 1), right=5 (value 11): sum=12 -- MATCH, return true immediately

sections.title: Tracing hasPairTwoPointers({1,3,5,7,9,11}, 25) -- no pair sums this high
steps: left=0 (1), right=5 (11): sum=12 < 25 -- too small, move left forward: left=1
left=1 (3), right=5 (11): sum=14 < 25 -- move left forward: left=2
left=2 (5), right=5 (11): sum=16 < 25 -- move left forward: left=3
left=3 (7), right=5 (11): sum=18 < 25 -- move left forward: left=4
left=4 (9), right=5 (11): sum=20 < 25 -- move left forward: left=5
left=5, right=5: left < right is now false -- loop ends, return false

sections.text: This algorithm ONLY works because the array is SORTED. If sum is too small, moving left forward can only increase it (since values increase left to right); if sum is too large, moving right backward can only decrease it. This directional guarantee is exactly what an unsorted array would break.

sections.title: Why two pointers beats brute force here
points: left only ever moves forward, right only ever moves backward
Combined, they can move AT MOST n times total before crossing (left >= right)
One single pass through the array, O(n), instead of checking every pair, O(n^2)

sections.text: 4. Removing Duplicates from a Sorted Array (Same-Direction Pointers)

This is a DIFFERENT two-pointer pattern: slow and fast both move forward, but at different rates. fast scans every element; slow only advances (and overwrites arr[slow]) when a genuinely new value is found. This modifies the array in place, achieving O(n) with no extra array needed.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using the opposite-direction two-pointer pattern on an UNSORTED array -- the technique relies entirely on sorted order to know which pointer to move.
Forgetting the loop condition should be left < right (not <=) for opposite-direction pointers, to avoid comparing an element with itself or crossing incorrectly.
Confusing the two different two-pointer patterns -- opposite-direction (converging inward) vs same-direction at different speeds (slow/fast) -- they solve different kinds of problems.
Reaching for nested loops out of habit when a sorted array and a two-pointer approach would solve the same problem in O(n) instead of O(n^2).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the two-pointer technique?
questions.answer: An approach using two index variables moving through a data structure -- either converging from opposite ends, or moving in the same direction at different speeds -- to solve a problem in a single O(n) pass instead of nested O(n^2) loops.

questions.question: Why does the opposite-direction two-pointer pair-sum approach require a sorted array?
questions.answer: Because moving left forward only increases the sum (since values increase left to right in sorted order), and moving right backward only decreases it -- this directional guarantee is what lets the algorithm safely decide which pointer to move at each step.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of the opposite-direction two-pointer approach, and why?
questions.answer: O(n). left only ever moves forward and right only ever moves backward, so together they can move at most n times total before the pointers cross, giving a single overall pass through the array.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Two pointers uses two index variables moving through an array to avoid nested loops. One common pattern converges two pointers from opposite ends inward -- for pair-sum on a SORTED array, moving the left pointer forward increases the sum and moving the right pointer backward decreases it, letting the algorithm solve the problem in O(n) instead of the brute-force O(n^2). Another pattern moves two pointers in the same direction at different speeds (slow/fast), useful for tasks like removing duplicates from a sorted array in place. Both patterns replace nested-loop brute force with a single efficient pass.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Two pointers = two hands reaching into a sorted line from both ends, walking toward each other, OR one hand scouting ahead (fast) while the other marks progress (slow). Either way: one honest pass, O(n), no nested loops needed.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why does the opposite-direction pair-sum technique require a sorted array?
What is the time complexity of the two-pointer pair-sum approach?
What's the difference between the opposite-direction and same-direction (slow/fast) two-pointer patterns?
What would happen if you used the pair-sum two-pointer technique on an unsorted array?
